docs.title: Learn BrickflowUI
docs.text:
This learning guide is a practical curriculum for becoming productive with BrickflowUI.

It is not an API reference. It is a guided path that teaches the mental model, the common patterns, the sharp edges, and the way experienced users build real apps with the library.

The guide assumes you already know Python. It does not assume you know React, frontend build systems, WebSockets, Databricks Apps, or dashboard design.

docs.title: What You Will Learn
docs.text:
By the end, you should be able to build:

  • a simple interactive app
  • a multi-section dashboard
  • a filtered table and chart experience
  • a pipeline observability portal
  • a chatbot-style assistant UI
  • a polished landing page
  • a Databricks-ready app package
  • a full capstone app that combines all of the above

docs.title: Recommended Path
docs.text:
Follow the chapters in order the first time:

  1. What Is BrickflowUI
  2. Install And Run
  3. Your First App
  4. Mental Model
  5. Layout Skills
  6. Components And Composition
  7. State And Events
  8. Inputs And User Actions
  9. Forms And API Routes
  10. Tables And Data Display
  11. Charts And Visualizations
  12. Pipeline Dashboards
  13. Chatbot And Assistant UI
  14. Theming And Branding
  15. Navigation And App Structure
  16. Databricks Apps Deployment
  17. Debugging And Reliability
  18. Production Checklist
  19. Capstone Project
  20. Glossary

docs.title: How To Use This Guide
docs.text:
Each chapter has:

  • a learning goal
  • a practical explanation
  • copy-paste code
  • common mistakes
  • exercises
  • a checkpoint

Do not rush the exercises. BrickflowUI skill comes from understanding how state, events, data, layout, and visuals fit together.

docs.title: The Big Idea
docs.text:
BrickflowUI lets you describe UI in Python:

return db.Column(
    [
        db.Text("Pipeline Health", variant="h1"),
        db.Button("Refresh", on_click=refresh),
    ]
)

The framework renders that Python component tree through a bundled frontend, listens for user events, calls your Python handlers, and sends UI updates back to the browser.

You write Python. BrickflowUI handles the browser runtime.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Understand what BrickflowUI is, what it is good for, and how it differs from notebooks, Streamlit-style rerun apps, and hand-written frontend apps.

docs.title: What BrickflowUI Is
docs.text:
BrickflowUI is a Python UI framework for building dashboards, portals, internal tools, Databricks Apps, and data products.

You build pages from Python functions:

import brickflowui as db

app = db.App(title="Ops Portal")

@app.page("/", title="Home")
def home():
    return db.Text("Hello BrickflowUI", variant="h1")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()

The UI is not a notebook cell. It is an app with pages, layout components, controls, state, charts, tables, API routes, packaged frontend assets, and Databricks-friendly runtime behavior.

docs.title: When To Use It
docs.text:
Use BrickflowUI when you want to build:

  • data engineering dashboards
  • pipeline command centers
  • admin portals
  • approval workflows
  • lightweight internal apps
  • assistant or chatbot workspaces
  • Databricks Apps backed by SQL or Unity Catalog

docs.title: When Not To Use It
docs.text:
BrickflowUI may not be the right fit when:

  • you need a public consumer-scale website with custom frontend engineering
  • you need pixel-perfect marketing animation controlled by frontend specialists
  • you need a full React codebase with custom client-side routing
  • you only need a one-off notebook chart

docs.title: The Mental Shift
docs.text:
Instead of thinking:

I need to write HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and backend APIs.

Think:

I need to return a tree of Python components, then update state when users interact.

That tree is called a VNode tree.

docs.title: A Tiny Example
docs.text:
@app.page("/")
def home():
    count, set_count = db.use_state(0)

    return db.Card(
        [
            db.Text("Counter", variant="h2"),
            db.Text(f"Current value: {count}"),
            db.Button("Increment", on_click=lambda: set_count(count + 1)),
        ]
    )

What happens:

  1. Python renders the component tree.
  2. The browser displays it.
  3. The user clicks the button.
  4. BrickflowUI calls your Python handler.
  5. State changes.
  6. The changed UI is sent back to the browser.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Treating BrickflowUI like a notebook instead of an app framework.
  • Putting too much business logic directly inside UI code.
  • Forgetting that every interactive value should be connected to state.
  • Expecting custom frontend files to be required for normal dashboards.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Write down one app you want to build with BrickflowUI. Classify it as a dashboard, portal, pipeline command center, chatbot workspace, landing page, or workflow app.

Then list the first three components you think it needs.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should now understand that BrickflowUI is a Python-first app framework that renders interactive browser UIs without requiring you to write frontend code.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Install BrickflowUI, run a local app, and understand which files are needed for a Databricks App.

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
Use:

pip install brickflowui

For local development from this repository:

pip install -e ".[dev]"

For docs work:

pip install -e ".[docs]"

If mkdocs is not recognized on Windows, prefer:

python -m mkdocs serve

docs.title: Minimal App File
docs.text:
Create app.py:

import brickflowui as db

app = db.App(title="Learning App")

@app.page("/", title="Home")
def home():
    return db.Text("BrickflowUI is running", variant="h1")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()

Run:

python app.py

Open:

http://127.0.0.1:8050

docs.title: Databricks App Files
docs.text:
A Databricks App normally needs:

app.py
app.yaml
requirements.txt

Example requirements.txt:

brickflowui>=0.1.6

Example app.yaml:

command:
  - python
  - app.py

env:
  - name: BRICKFLOWUI_ENV
    value: production

docs.title: Local Repo Development
docs.text:
The Python package serves pre-built frontend files from:

brickflowui/frontend/dist/

If you change files under frontend/src, rebuild:

cd frontend
npm run build

Then commit the updated brickflowui/frontend/dist assets.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Installing brickflowui in one Python environment and running the app from another.
  • Editing frontend source but forgetting to rebuild bundled assets.
  • Deleting brickflowui/frontend/dist before packaging.
  • Pushing frontend/node_modules to git.
  • Expecting root dist/ or site/ to be source files. They are generated.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Create a fresh folder with app.py, requirements.txt, and app.yaml.

Run it locally. Then change the title and confirm the browser updates after restart.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to install BrickflowUI, run a local app, and explain why brickflowui/frontend/dist is part of the package.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Build a small interactive app with state, layout, text, and a button.

docs.title: Start With A Page
docs.text:
import brickflowui as db

app = db.App(title="First BrickflowUI App")

@app.page("/", title="Home")
def home():
    return db.Text("Hello from BrickflowUI", variant="h1")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()

Pages are normal Python functions decorated with @app.page(...).

docs.title: Add Layout
docs.text:
@app.page("/", title="Home")
def home():
    return db.Column(
        [
            db.Text("Hello from BrickflowUI", variant="h1"),
            db.Text("This is my first page."),
        ],
        gap=4,
        padding=6,
    )

Column stacks components vertically.

docs.title: Add State
docs.text:
@app.page("/", title="Home")
def home():
    count, set_count = db.use_state(0)

    return db.Card(
        [
            db.Text("Counter", variant="h2"),
            db.Text(f"Count: {count}"),
            db.Button("Increment", on_click=lambda: set_count(count + 1)),
        ],
        elevated=True,
    )

State makes the page interactive.

docs.title: Complete First App
docs.text:
import brickflowui as db

app = db.App(title="First BrickflowUI App")

@app.page("/", title="Home")
def home():
    count, set_count = db.use_state(0)

    return db.Column(
        [
            db.Text("My First BrickflowUI App", variant="h1"),
            db.Card(
                [
                    db.Text("Counter", variant="h2"),
                    db.Text(f"You clicked {count} times."),
                    db.Button("Increment", on_click=lambda: set_count(count + 1)),
                ],
                elevated=True,
                animated=True,
            ),
        ],
        gap=4,
        padding=6,
    )

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Calling set_count(count + 1) immediately instead of inside lambda.
  • Returning a raw list instead of a component.
  • Forgetting if __name__ == "__main__": app.run().
  • Expecting state to be shared across all users. State is session-oriented.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Add two more buttons:

  • one that decrements the count
  • one that resets the count to zero

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should now be able to create a page, compose layout, and update UI from a button click.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Understand the component tree, render cycle, event flow, and how BrickflowUI keeps Python and the browser connected.

docs.title: Component Tree
docs.text:
Every BrickflowUI component returns a VNode.

db.Text("Hello")
db.Button("Run")
db.Card([db.Text("Inside")])

When you return a layout:

return db.Column(
    [
        db.Text("A"),
        db.Text("B"),
    ]
)

you are returning a tree:

Column
  Text
  Text

docs.title: Render Cycle
docs.text:
The render cycle is:

  1. Browser connects to the app runtime.
  2. Python calls the current page function.
  3. The page function returns a VNode tree.
  4. The tree is serialized to JSON.
  5. The frontend renders the JSON.
  6. User events are sent back to Python.
  7. Python handlers update state.
  8. BrickflowUI sends a patch back to the browser.

docs.title: Events
docs.text:
When you write:

db.Button("Refresh", on_click=refresh)

BrickflowUI registers an event handler. The browser does not know your Python function. It knows an event id. When clicked, the event id is sent to Python, and Python calls refresh.

docs.title: State
docs.text:
State is what lets the next render remember something.

value, set_value = db.use_state("all")

When set_value("gold") runs, the page renders again with value == "gold".

docs.title: Why This Matters
docs.text:
If your UI does not update, ask:

  • Is the value stored in state?
  • Is the input connected to a setter?
  • Does the component receive the current state value?
  • Is the event handler being called?
  • Is the page returning the new value?

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@app.page("/")
def home():
    layer, set_layer = db.use_state("bronze")

    return db.Column(
        [
            db.Select(
                name="layer",
                label="Layer",
                value=layer,
                options=[
                    {"label": "Bronze", "value": "bronze"},
                    {"label": "Silver", "value": "silver"},
                    {"label": "Gold", "value": "gold"},
                ],
                on_change=set_layer,
            ),
            db.Text(f"Selected layer: {layer}"),
        ]
    )

The select value and text are both driven by the same state.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Creating local variables and expecting them to survive user events.
  • Updating a variable instead of state.
  • Passing a fixed value to an input instead of the current state value.
  • Mutating lists or dictionaries in place and expecting the UI to notice.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text: Create a page with a Select and a Badge. The badge should show the currently selected environment: dev, staging, or prod.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to explain the path from Python component tree to browser event and back to Python state.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Use BrickflowUI layout primitives to create readable, responsive pages.

docs.title: Core Layout Components
docs.text:
The most important layout components are:

  • Column
  • Row
  • Grid
  • Card
  • Spacer
  • Divider

docs.title: Column
docs.text:
Use Column for vertical structure.

db.Column(
    [
        db.Text("Title", variant="h1"),
        db.Text("Subtitle"),
    ],
    gap=4,
    padding=6,
)

docs.title: Row
docs.text:
Use Row for horizontal alignment.

db.Row(
    [
        db.Text("Pipeline Health", variant="h2"),
        db.Button("Refresh"),
    ],
    justify="between",
    align="center",
)

docs.title: Grid
docs.text:
Use Grid for dashboards.

db.Grid(
    [
        db.Card([db.Stat("Runs", "96")]),
        db.Card([db.Stat("Success", "99.1%")]),
        db.Card([db.Stat("Freshness", "11m")]),
    ],
    cols=3,
    gap=4,
)

On smaller screens, the grid collapses responsively.

docs.title: Card
docs.text:
Use Card to group related content.

db.Card(
    [
        db.Text("Freshness", variant="h3"),
        db.Progress(88, label="Freshness score"),
    ],
    elevated=True,
    hover=True,
)

docs.title: Page Skeleton Pattern
docs.text:
Most apps can start with:

return db.Column(
    [
        db.Row(
            [
                db.Column(
                    [
                        db.Text("Dashboard", variant="h1"),
                        db.Text("Operational overview", muted=True),
                    ]
                ),
                db.Button("Refresh"),
            ],
            justify="between",
        ),
        db.Grid(kpi_cards, cols=4),
        db.Grid(chart_cards, cols=2),
        db.Card([table]),
    ],
    gap=5,
    padding=6,
)

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Using too many nested Row components when a Grid would be clearer.
  • Putting every component in its own Card.
  • Forgetting wrap=True for rows that may overflow on small screens.
  • Using large padding everywhere instead of creating a page-level padding once.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text: Build a dashboard shell with a title row, four KPI cards, two chart cards, and one table card.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to create structured pages with clear vertical rhythm, responsive grids, and grouped surfaces.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Learn how to create reusable Python helper functions that return BrickflowUI components.

docs.title: Why Compose
docs.text:
If you write every card inline, pages become hard to read.

Instead of repeating:

db.Card(
    [
        db.Stat(label="Runs", value="96", delta="+4%"),
        db.Text("Healthy run volume", muted=True),
    ]
)

create a helper:

def metric_card(label: str, value: str, delta: str, note: str):
    return db.Card(
        [
            db.Stat(label=label, value=value, delta=delta, delta_type="increase"),
            db.Text(note, variant="caption", muted=True),
        ],
        elevated=True,
        hover=True,
    )

Then use:

db.Grid(
    [
        metric_card("Runs", "96", "+4%", "Healthy run volume"),
        metric_card("Success", "99.1%", "+0.3 pts", "Stable reliability"),
    ],
    cols=2,
)

docs.title: Component Helpers Are Just Python
docs.text:
You can use parameters, if statements, loops, list comprehensions, and data transformations.

Example:

def status_badge(status: str):
    color = {
        "healthy": "green",
        "watch": "orange",
        "failed": "red",
    }.get(status.lower(), "gray")

    return db.Badge(status, color=color)

docs.title: Pattern: View Functions
docs.text:
For larger apps, split sections into functions:

def overview_view(rows):
    return db.Column(
        [
            db.Text("Overview", variant="h1"),
            db.Grid([...], cols=4),
        ]
    )

def detail_view(rows):
    return db.Column(
        [
            db.Text("Details", variant="h1"),
            db.Table(rows),
        ]
    )

Then switch between them:

view, set_view = db.use_state("overview")
content = overview_view(rows) if view == "overview" else detail_view(rows)
return db.Column([nav, content])

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Hiding too much state inside helper functions.
  • Making helpers depend on global mutable state.
  • Returning lists from helpers instead of a single VNode when a VNode is expected.
  • Creating helpers that are too generic too early.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Create three helpers:

  • metric_card
  • section_header
  • pipeline_badge

Use them in a small page.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should now be able to make BrickflowUI code readable by composing UI with Python helper functions.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Build interactive pages where user actions update state and re-render the UI.

docs.title: use_state
docs.text:
State is declared inside a page:

value, set_value = db.use_state("initial")

Use the value in your UI:

db.Text(f"Current value: {value}")

Update it from an event:

db.Button("Change", on_click=lambda: set_value("changed"))

docs.title: Event Handler Shapes
docs.text:
Different components send different values:

  • Button.on_click receives no required value.
  • Input.on_change receives a string.
  • Select.on_change receives a string.
  • Checkbox.on_change receives a boolean.
  • Slider.on_change receives a number.
  • DateRangePicker.on_change receives {"start": "...", "end": "..."}.
  • MultiSelect.on_change receives list[str].
  • Table.on_row_click receives the row dictionary.
  • PipelineGraph.on_node_click receives the node dictionary.
  • ChatInput.on_submit receives the submitted text.

docs.title: Example: Filter State
docs.text:
@app.page("/")
def home():
    layer, set_layer = db.use_state("all")
    urgent_only, set_urgent_only = db.use_state(False)

    rows = [
        {"pipeline": "Orders", "layer": "silver", "status": "watch"},
        {"pipeline": "Finance", "layer": "gold", "status": "healthy"},
    ]

    filtered = [
        row
        for row in rows
        if (layer == "all" or row["layer"] == layer)
        and (not urgent_only or row["status"] == "watch")
    ]

    return db.Column(
        [
            db.Select(
                name="layer",
                label="Layer",
                value=layer,
                options=[
                    {"label": "All", "value": "all"},
                    {"label": "Silver", "value": "silver"},
                    {"label": "Gold", "value": "gold"},
                ],
                on_change=set_layer,
            ),
            db.Checkbox(
                name="urgent",
                label="Urgent only",
                checked=urgent_only,
                on_change=set_urgent_only,
            ),
            db.Table(filtered),
        ]
    )

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Forgetting to pass value=state into controlled inputs.
  • Forgetting to pass checked=state into checkbox/toggle controls.
  • Calling setters during render instead of inside callbacks.
  • Mutating a state list directly.

Bad:

items.append("new")
set_items(items)

Better:

set_items([*items, "new"])

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text: Create a page with a search input, status select, and table. The table should filter when either control changes.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should understand how user events flow into state and how state drives the next render.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Use input components safely so user-provided values become useful app state.

docs.title: Inputs Are Controlled
docs.text:
A controlled input receives its current value from state and sends changes back through an event.

query, set_query = db.use_state("")

db.Input(
    name="query",
    label="Search",
    value=query,
    on_change=set_query,
)

docs.title: Select
docs.text:
layer, set_layer = db.use_state("all")

db.Select(
    name="layer",
    label="Layer",
    value=layer,
    options=[
        {"label": "All", "value": "all"},
        {"label": "Bronze", "value": "bronze"},
        {"label": "Silver", "value": "silver"},
        {"label": "Gold", "value": "gold"},
    ],
    on_change=set_layer,
)

docs.title: Checkbox
docs.text:
urgent_only, set_urgent_only = db.use_state(False)

db.Checkbox(
    name="urgent",
    label="Only show urgent items",
    checked=urgent_only,
    on_change=set_urgent_only,
)

docs.title: Slider
docs.text:
min_success, set_min_success = db.use_state(95.0)

db.Slider(
    name="min_success",
    label=f"Minimum success: {min_success:.1f}%",
    min=90,
    max=100,
    step=0.1,
    value=min_success,
    on_change=set_min_success,
)

docs.title: Date Range
docs.text:
window, set_window = db.use_state({"start": "2026-04-01", "end": "2026-04-07"})

db.DateRangePicker(
    name="window",
    label="Run window",
    start=window["start"],
    end=window["end"],
    on_change=set_window,
)

docs.title: Multi Select
docs.text:
layers, set_layers = db.use_state(["bronze", "silver"])

db.MultiSelect(
    name="layers",
    label="Layers",
    values=layers,
    options=[
        {"label": "Bronze", "value": "bronze"},
        {"label": "Silver", "value": "silver"},
        {"label": "Gold", "value": "gold"},
    ],
    on_change=set_layers,
)

docs.title: Chat Input
docs.text:
prompt, set_prompt = db.use_state("")
answer, set_answer = db.use_state("Ask a question.")

db.ChatInput(
    value=prompt,
    on_change=set_prompt,
    on_submit=lambda text: set_answer(f"You asked: {text}"),
)

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Missing name on inputs used in forms.
  • Treating slider values as strings when your logic expects numbers.
  • Letting input state and displayed values drift apart.
  • Forgetting that DateRangePicker returns a dictionary.
  • Forgetting that MultiSelect returns a list.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Build a control panel with:

  • search input
  • layer select
  • urgent checkbox
  • success-rate slider
  • date range picker

Show the current values below the controls.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to collect user input, store it in state, and use it to drive UI.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Use forms for backend submissions and custom API routes for app-specific actions.

docs.title: When To Use Forms
docs.text:
Use Form when a user submits structured data:

  • action plan
  • approval request
  • feedback
  • job configuration
  • support ticket

Use direct component callbacks when the action is mainly UI state:

  • changing filters
  • opening a drawer
  • selecting a row
  • switching views

docs.title: Basic Form
docs.text:
db.Form(
    [
        db.Input(name="owner", label="Owner"),
        db.Input(name="summary", label="Summary"),
        db.Button("Submit", html_type="submit"),
    ],
    action="/api/action-plan",
    method="POST",
)

docs.title: API Route
docs.text:
@app.route("/api/action-plan", methods=["POST"])
async def create_action_plan(request):
    payload = await request.json()
    return {
        "status": "ok",
        "owner": payload.get("owner"),
        "summary": payload.get("summary"),
    }

docs.title: Full Example
docs.text:
import brickflowui as db

app = db.App()

@app.route("/api/feedback", methods=["POST"])
async def feedback(request):
    payload = await request.json()
    return {"status": "received", "message": payload.get("message", "")}

@app.page("/")
def home():
    return db.Card(
        [
            db.Text("Feedback", variant="h2"),
            db.Form(
                [
                    db.Input(name="name", label="Name"),
                    db.Input(name="message", label="Message", type="textarea"),
                    db.Button("Send", html_type="submit"),
                ],
                action="/api/feedback",
                method="POST",
            ),
        ]
    )

docs.title: Form Serialization
docs.text: Inputs with the same name become arrays. This matters for multi-value fields.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Forgetting html_type="submit" on the submit button.
  • Expecting Form to update page state automatically.
  • Using forms for every interaction when simple callbacks are clearer.
  • Forgetting await request.json() in async route handlers.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Create an action-plan form with owner, priority, summary, and due date.

Return a JSON response from /api/action-plan.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should know when to use component callbacks and when to use form submissions/API routes.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Display structured data with tables, badges, empty states, loading states, and row drilldowns.

docs.title: Basic Table
docs.text:
rows = [
    {"pipeline": "Orders", "status": "Healthy", "freshness": 12},
    {"pipeline": "Finance", "status": "Watch", "freshness": 47},
]

db.Table(rows)

docs.title: Explicit Columns
docs.text:
db.Table(
    rows,
    columns=[
        {"key": "pipeline", "label": "Pipeline", "sortable": True},
        {"key": "status", "label": "Status", "sortable": True},
        {"key": "freshness", "label": "Freshness", "sortable": True},
    ],
)

docs.title: Empty State
docs.text:
db.Table(
    [],
    columns=[{"key": "pipeline", "label": "Pipeline"}],
    empty_message="No pipelines match your filters.",
)

docs.title: Export CSV
docs.text:
db.Table(rows, exportable=True)

This adds a CSV export button in the browser.

docs.title: Row Click
docs.text:
selected, set_selected = db.use_state(None)

db.Table(
    rows,
    columns=[...],
    on_row_click=set_selected,
)

Then render selected details:

if selected:
    detail = db.Alert(f"Selected {selected['pipeline']}", type="info")
else:
    detail = db.EmptyState("No row selected", "Click a row to inspect it.")

docs.title: Badge Pattern
docs.text:
def status_badge(status: str):
    color = {
        "Healthy": "green",
        "Watch": "orange",
        "Failed": "red",
    }.get(status, "gray")

    return db.Badge(status, color=color)

Tables currently display plain values, so use badges in cards, drawers, or detail sections next to tables.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Letting tables grow too wide without using focused columns.
  • Showing raw technical column names to business users.
  • Forgetting empty states.
  • Hiding export behind custom code when exportable=True is enough.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Create a table of pipeline runs with pipeline, layer, owner, status, freshness, and cost.

Add row click that displays the selected row in an alert or card.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to render useful operational tables with sorting, pagination, empty states, export, and drilldowns.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Choose the right chart component and connect chart interactions to state.

docs.title: Chart Components
docs.text:
BrickflowUI includes:

  • AreaChart
  • LineChart
  • BarChart
  • DonutChart
  • ScatterChart
  • ComposedChart
  • GaugeChart
  • RadarChart
  • Heatmap
  • FunnelChart
  • TreeMap
  • SparklineStat

docs.title: Area Chart
docs.text:
Use for trends with volume.

db.AreaChart(
    data=[
        {"day": "Mon", "rows": 120},
        {"day": "Tue", "rows": 148},
    ],
    x_key="day",
    y_keys=["rows"],
    title="Rows Processed",
)

docs.title: Composed Chart
docs.text:
Use when you need multiple signals in one chart.

db.ComposedChart(
    data=[
        {"day": "Mon", "runs": 85, "success": 98.6, "cost": 310},
        {"day": "Tue", "runs": 89, "success": 98.9, "cost": 328},
    ],
    x_key="day",
    bar_keys=["runs"],
    line_keys=["success"],
    area_keys=["cost"],
    title="Runs, Success, and Cost",
)

docs.title: Gauge Chart
docs.text:
Use for one important score.

db.GaugeChart(97.8, label="Reliability score")

docs.title: Heatmap
docs.text:
Use for failures, latency, or freshness by time and layer.

db.Heatmap(
    data=[
        {"hour": "08", "layer": "bronze", "failures": 0},
        {"hour": "08", "layer": "silver", "failures": 2},
    ],
    x_key="hour",
    y_key="layer",
    value_key="failures",
    title="Failures by Hour and Layer",
)

docs.title: Chart Clicks
docs.text:
Many charts support on_click:

selected, set_selected = db.use_state("No point selected")

db.ScatterChart(
    rows,
    x_key="cost",
    y_key="duration",
    title="Cost vs Duration",
    on_click=lambda point: set_selected(point["pipeline"]),
)

docs.title: Loading And Empty States
docs.text:
db.BarChart(
    data=rows,
    x_key="week",
    y_keys=["runs"],
    loading=is_loading,
    empty_message="No run data available.",
)

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Using too many chart types on one page.
  • Charting unfiltered data while the table shows filtered data.
  • Forgetting units in titles or labels.
  • Making every chart clickable without a useful drilldown.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Build a page with:

  • a GaugeChart for success rate
  • a ComposedChart for runs and cost
  • a Heatmap for failures
  • a Table showing the same filtered rows

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to choose chart types based on the question your dashboard needs to answer.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Build a pipeline command center with graph structure, health signals, filters, charts, and drilldowns.

docs.title: Pipeline Dashboard Anatomy
docs.text:
A useful pipeline dashboard usually has:

  • health summary
  • freshness or SLA score
  • pipeline graph
  • failure or latency trend
  • table of runs
  • owner/team context
  • drilldown for selected node or row
  • triage queue

docs.title: Pipeline Graph
docs.text:
nodes = [
    {"id": "source", "label": "Source API", "layer": "source", "status": "healthy"},
    {"id": "bronze", "label": "Bronze Events", "layer": "bronze", "status": "running"},
    {"id": "silver", "label": "Silver Quality", "layer": "silver", "status": "watch"},
    {"id": "gold", "label": "Gold Marts", "layer": "gold", "status": "healthy"},
]

edges = [
    {"from": "source", "to": "bronze"},
    {"from": "bronze", "to": "silver"},
    {"from": "silver", "to": "gold"},
]

selected, set_selected = db.use_state("No node selected")

db.PipelineGraph(
    nodes=nodes,
    edges=edges,
    title="Lakehouse Flow",
    on_node_click=lambda node: set_selected(f"{node['label']} selected"),
)

docs.title: Status Strip
docs.text:
db.StatusStrip(
    [
        {"label": "Pipelines", "value": "12", "status": "info"},
        {"label": "Success", "value": "99.1%", "status": "healthy"},
        {"label": "Freshness", "value": "18m", "status": "healthy"},
        {"label": "Incidents", "value": "2", "status": "watch"},
    ]
)

docs.title: Triage Board
docs.text:
db.KanbanBoard(
    [
        {"id": "healthy", "label": "Healthy", "cards": []},
        {"id": "watch", "label": "Watch", "cards": [{"id": "orders", "title": "Orders Lakehouse"}]},
        {"id": "risk", "label": "At Risk", "cards": [{"id": "ml", "title": "ML Features"}]},
    ],
    on_card_click=lambda card: set_selected(card["title"]),
)

docs.title: Recommended Data Shape
docs.text:
Start with rows like:

{
    "pipeline": "Orders Lakehouse",
    "layer": "silver",
    "status": "watch",
    "freshness": 44,
    "success": 97.8,
    "rows_m": 84,
    "cost": 127,
    "duration": 36,
}

This shape is simple enough for mock data and easy to replace with Databricks SQL results.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Showing a graph with no table for operational details.
  • Showing a table with no summary of what matters.
  • Forgetting owners and runbook context.
  • Mixing pipeline topology with run history without labels.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Build a command center with:

  • StatusStrip
  • PipelineGraph
  • GaugeChart
  • ComposedChart
  • Table
  • KanbanBoard

All should use the same mock pipeline data.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to represent data pipelines as both operational metrics and flow structure.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Build assistant-style interfaces that work alongside dashboards.

docs.title: Why Add A Chat UI
docs.text:
An assistant UI is useful when users want to ask:

  • why is this pipeline delayed?
  • which dataset is stale?
  • what changed since yesterday?
  • what should I investigate first?
  • summarize the current incidents

BrickflowUI gives you the building blocks. You decide whether the response comes from simple rules, an API route, a model, or a Databricks job.

docs.title: Basic Chat
docs.text:
prompt, set_prompt = db.use_state("")
answer, set_answer = db.use_state("Ask me about pipeline health.")

db.Column(
    [
        db.ChatMessage("assistant", answer, name="Ops Copilot"),
        db.ChatInput(
            value=prompt,
            on_change=set_prompt,
            on_submit=lambda text: set_answer(f"Investigating: {text}"),
        ),
    ],
    gap=4,
)

docs.title: Chat With Dashboard Context
docs.text:
rows = filtered_rows

def answer_question(text: str):
    if "failed" in text.lower():
        failed = [row for row in rows if row["status"] == "failed"]
        set_answer(f"{len(failed)} pipelines failed in the current filter.")
    else:
        set_answer(f"{len(rows)} pipelines match the current dashboard filters.")

Then:

db.ChatInput(value=prompt, on_change=set_prompt, on_submit=answer_question)

docs.title: Assistant Layout Pattern
docs.text:
db.Card(
    [
        db.SectionHeader("Pipeline Copilot", "Ask questions about the filtered dashboard state."),
        db.ChatMessage("assistant", answer, name="Ops Copilot"),
        db.ChatInput(value=prompt, on_change=set_prompt, on_submit=answer_question),
    ],
    elevated=True,
)

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Building a chat box that ignores current dashboard filters.
  • Not storing prompt text in state.
  • Not clearing or updating the prompt after submit when desired.
  • Making the assistant sound authoritative when the data is incomplete.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Create a chat card that answers:

  • "what is at risk?"
  • "what costs most?"
  • "how many pipelines are healthy?"

Use simple Python logic over mock rows.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to create a useful assistant UI that is grounded in dashboard state.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Make apps feel intentional and branded without writing custom frontend code.

docs.title: Theme Object
docs.text:
You can pass a theme dictionary to App:

app = db.App(
    theme={
        "branding": {"title": "Astellas Pipeline Studio"},
        "colors": {
            "primary": "#C81E5B",
            "primary_hover": "#A8184A",
            "background": "#F7F8F7",
            "surface": "#FFFFFF",
            "text": "#1B1F1D",
            "text_muted": "#5E6A64",
            "border": "#E2E8E3",
            "success": "#0F8A6C",
            "warning": "#C67A00",
            "error": "#B42318",
            "info": "#2563EB",
        },
        "borders": {"radius": "18px"},
    }
)

docs.title: What Branding Means
docs.text:
Branding is not just colors. A polished app also needs:

  • clear typography
  • spacing rhythm
  • consistent surfaces
  • restrained motion
  • meaningful badges
  • useful empty states
  • action hierarchy

docs.title: Hero Pattern
docs.text:
db.Hero(
    title="Pipeline observability for serious internal data apps",
    subtitle="Monitor freshness, reliability, and cost from one workspace.",
    eyebrow="Data Platform",
    badges=[db.Badge("Live", color="green")],
    actions=[db.Button("Refresh"), db.Button("Open runbook", variant="secondary")],
)

docs.title: Card Polish
docs.text:
db.Card(
    [
        db.Stat("Success", "99.1%", delta="+0.3 pts", delta_type="increase"),
        db.Text("Stable reliability across selected pipelines.", variant="caption", muted=True),
    ],
    elevated=True,
    hover=True,
    animated=True,
)

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Using brand colors everywhere.
  • Mixing too many badge colors.
  • Making every card elevated.
  • Ignoring empty states and loading states.
  • Choosing visual polish before information hierarchy.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Create two themes:

  • a calm enterprise theme
  • a high-energy command-center theme

Apply both to the same dashboard and compare readability.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to make a BrickflowUI app feel branded, clear, and intentional.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Structure larger apps with pages, tabs, view state, sidebars, drawers, and modals.

docs.title: Multiple Pages
docs.text:
@app.page("/", title="Overview")
def overview():
    return db.Text("Overview", variant="h1")

@app.page("/pipelines", title="Pipelines")
def pipelines():
    return db.Text("Pipelines", variant="h1")

Use pages when the URL should represent a different screen.

docs.title: State-Based Views
docs.text:
Use state when views are part of one workflow:

view, set_view = db.use_state("overview")

nav = db.Row(
    [
        db.Button("Overview", on_click=lambda: set_view("overview")),
        db.Button("Health", on_click=lambda: set_view("health")),
    ]
)

content = overview_view() if view == "overview" else health_view()

return db.Column([nav, content])

docs.title: Tabs
docs.text:
Use tabs for related sub-sections:

db.Tabs(
    [
        db.TabItem("Runs", [db.Table(runs)]),
        db.TabItem("Incidents", [db.Table(incidents)]),
    ]
)

docs.title: Drawers
docs.text:
Use drawers for drilldowns:

open_drawer, set_open_drawer = db.use_state(False)

db.Drawer(
    visible=open_drawer,
    title="Pipeline Details",
    on_close=lambda: set_open_drawer(False),
    children=[db.Text("Details go here")],
)

docs.title: Modals
docs.text:
Use modals for focused actions:

  • confirm
  • submit
  • acknowledge
  • create action plan

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Creating too many pages when tabs would be simpler.
  • Hiding important dashboard context inside modals.
  • Using a modal for information users need to compare.
  • Not giving drawers an obvious close action.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text: Create a page with top navigation buttons, three state-based views, a row-click drawer, and a modal for creating an action plan.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to choose between pages, tabs, state views, drawers, and modals.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Prepare a BrickflowUI app for Databricks Apps and understand what files and settings matter.

docs.title: Required Files
docs.text:
Use this structure:

my_app/
  app.py
  app.yaml
  requirements.txt

docs.title: requirements.txt
docs.text:
brickflowui>=0.1.6

If using Databricks SQL or Unity Catalog helpers:

brickflowui[databricks]>=0.1.6

docs.title: app.yaml
docs.text:
command:
  - python
  - app.py

env:
  - name: BRICKFLOWUI_ENV
    value: production

BrickflowUI reads Databricks runtime environment variables and binds to the port Databricks provides.

docs.title: App Entry Point
docs.text:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()

Keep this in app.py.

docs.title: Databricks SQL Pattern
docs.text:
Start with mock data:

rows = [{"pipeline": "Orders", "status": "Healthy"}]

Then replace with SQL:

from brickflowui.databricks import sql

rows = sql.query_to_records("""
SELECT pipeline, status, freshness_min
FROM catalog.schema.pipeline_metrics
""")

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
• requirements.txt references the correct BrickflowUI version.
  • app.yaml starts python app.py.
  • The package installed in Databricks includes frontend assets.
  • The app does not require local files that were not uploaded.
  • Secrets and workspace-specific settings are read from environment variables.
  • The app has useful loading and empty states.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Installing an old PyPI version that does not include the needed frontend assets.
  • Forgetting to include requirements.txt.
  • Using a file path that exists locally but not in Databricks.
  • Assuming a cluster-installed library is the same as the app environment.
  • Debugging only the Python logs and not checking browser console/network.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text:
Take a local app and create requirements.txt and app.yaml.

Then explain what each file does.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should understand how to package a BrickflowUI app for Databricks Apps.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Diagnose common issues and make apps reliable.

docs.title: App Shows "Connecting To Runtime"
docs.text:
Check:

  • Is the Python server running?
  • Did the websocket connect?
  • Are frontend assets present in the installed package?
  • Is the app using the right requirements.txt version?
  • Are browser console errors related to CSP, missing assets, or websocket failure?

docs.title: Frontend Assets Missing
docs.text:
The package must include:

brickflowui/frontend/dist/index.html
brickflowui/frontend/dist/assets/*.js
brickflowui/frontend/dist/assets/*.css

If you changed frontend code:

cd frontend
npm run build

Then check the wheel:

python -m zipfile -l dist/brickflowui-0.1.6-py3-none-any.whl

docs.title: User Input Not Updating
docs.text:
Check:

  • Does the component receive value=... or checked=... from state?
  • Does it have on_change=...?
  • Is the setter updating the correct state variable?
  • Are you mutating a list/dict in place?

docs.title: Table Or Chart Empty
docs.text:
Check:

  • Are filters too strict?
  • Are data keys spelled correctly?
  • Does the chart use the same key names as the rows?
  • Did SQL return records in the expected shape?
  • Did you provide an empty_message?

docs.title: Build Issues
docs.text:
Run:

python -m pytest -q
cd frontend
npm run build
cd ..
python -m mkdocs build
python -m build
python -m twine check dist/brickflowui-0.1.6*

On Windows, prefer:

python -m mkdocs build

instead of relying on mkdocs being on PATH.

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Publishing without rebuilding frontend assets.
  • Forgetting to commit generated package assets.
  • Cleaning untracked files with git clean -fd and deleting new docs/examples before staging.
  • Treating root dist/ as source.
  • Pushing cache folders.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text: Create a broken chart by using the wrong y_key. Then fix it by matching the key to the data.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to debug connection, asset, state, data, and build issues methodically.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text: Prepare a BrickflowUI app for serious use by a team.

docs.title: UX Checklist
docs.text:
• Every page has a clear title.
  • Every control has a label.
  • Tables have useful column labels.
  • Charts have titles and units.
  • Empty states explain what happened.
  • Loading states are visible where data may be delayed.
  • Important actions are visually distinct.
  • Risk states use consistent colors.
  • The app works on laptop-sized screens.

docs.title: State Checklist
docs.text:
• Inputs are controlled by state.
  • Filters drive all relevant tables and charts.
  • Row and chart clicks produce useful drilldowns.
  • Multi-value controls use lists.
  • Date controls use a consistent shape.
  • State is not mutated in place.

docs.title: Data Checklist
docs.text:
• Mock data shape matches production data shape.
  • SQL result columns match component keys.
  • Expensive queries are not repeated unnecessarily.
  • Errors are surfaced clearly.
  • Sensitive data is not displayed accidentally.

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
• requirements.txt pins an appropriate version.
  • app.yaml is present.
  • Frontend assets are included in the installed package.
  • Environment variables are documented.
  • Databricks SQL access is tested.
  • Auth expectations are clear.

docs.title: Repository Checklist
docs.text:
Commit:

  • source files
  • docs
  • examples
  • tests
  • brickflowui/frontend/dist

Do not commit:

  • frontend/node_modules
  • root dist
  • site
  • __pycache__
  • .pytest_cache
  • pytest-cache-files-*
  • _tmp_cli_*
  • throwaway sample_app_*

docs.title: Release Checklist
docs.text:
Run:

python -m pytest -q
cd frontend
npm run build
cd ..
python -m mkdocs build
python -m build
python -m twine check dist/brickflowui-0.1.6*

docs.title: Common Mistakes
docs.text:
• Treating examples as an afterthought.
  • Shipping a new component without docs.
  • Shipping docs without running MkDocs.
  • Shipping frontend changes without checking the wheel.

docs.title: Exercise
docs.text: Take one example app and review it against this checklist. Write down the first five improvements you would make.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: You should be able to judge whether a BrickflowUI app is ready for team use.

docs.title: Learning Goal
docs.text:
Build one complete app that proves you can use BrickflowUI as a skill.

The capstone is a Data Product Command Center. It combines:

  • landing-page style hero
  • dashboard KPIs
  • filters
  • tables
  • multiple charts
  • pipeline graph
  • kanban triage
  • chatbot assistant
  • theming
  • Databricks deployment files

docs.title: App Concept
docs.text:
Your app helps a data platform team monitor pipelines and explain the current state to stakeholders.

It should answer:

  • Are pipelines healthy?
  • Which layer is delayed?
  • What is the success rate?
  • What costs the most?
  • Which pipeline should we investigate first?
  • What does the assistant say about the current filters?

docs.title: File Structure
docs.text:
capstone_command_center/
  app.py
  app.yaml
  requirements.txt

docs.title: Step 1: Create Mock Data
docs.text:
PIPELINES = [
    {"pipeline": "Customer 360", "layer": "gold", "status": "healthy", "freshness": 11, "success": 99.4, "cost": 82, "duration": 18},
    {"pipeline": "Orders Lakehouse", "layer": "silver", "status": "watch", "freshness": 44, "success": 97.8, "cost": 127, "duration": 36},
    {"pipeline": "ML Features", "layer": "silver", "status": "at risk", "freshness": 78, "success": 95.9, "cost": 119, "duration": 55},
]

docs.title: Step 2: Add Theme
docs.text:
app = db.App(
    theme={
        "branding": {"title": "Data Product Command Center"},
        "colors": {
            "primary": "#C81E5B",
            "primary_hover": "#A8184A",
            "background": "#F7F8F7",
            "surface": "#FFFFFF",
            "text": "#1B1F1D",
            "text_muted": "#5E6A64",
            "border": "#E2E8E3",
        },
        "borders": {"radius": "18px"},
    }
)

docs.title: Step 3: Add Controls
docs.text: Use Select for layer, Input for search, Slider for success-rate floor, and Checkbox for urgent-only.

docs.title: Step 4: Add Summary
docs.text: Use Hero, StatusStrip, GaugeChart, and Stat.

docs.title: Step 5: Add Charts
docs.text:
Use:

  • ComposedChart for runs, success, and cost
  • ScatterChart for cost vs duration
  • Heatmap for failures by hour/layer
  • TreeMap for cost concentration
  • RadarChart for platform scorecard
  • FunnelChart for pipeline stage gates

docs.title: Step 6: Add Pipeline Graph
docs.text:
db.PipelineGraph(
    nodes=[
        {"id": "source", "label": "Source", "status": "healthy"},
        {"id": "bronze", "label": "Bronze", "status": "running"},
        {"id": "silver", "label": "Silver", "status": "watch"},
        {"id": "gold", "label": "Gold", "status": "healthy"},
    ],
    edges=[
        {"from": "source", "to": "bronze"},
        {"from": "bronze", "to": "silver"},
        {"from": "silver", "to": "gold"},
    ],
    on_node_click=lambda node: set_selected(node["label"]),
)

docs.title: Step 7: Add Triage
docs.text:
Use KanbanBoard with Healthy, Watch, and At Risk columns.

Clicking a card should update selected detail state.

docs.title: Step 8: Add Assistant
docs.text:
Use ChatMessage and ChatInput.

The assistant should answer at least:

  • "what is at risk?"
  • "what costs most?"
  • "how many pipelines are healthy?"

It can use simple Python logic.

docs.title: Step 9: Add Table
docs.text:
Use:

db.Table(filtered_rows, exportable=True, empty_message="No pipelines match your filters.")

docs.title: Step 10: Add Deployment Files
docs.text:
requirements.txt:

brickflowui>=0.1.6

app.yaml:

command:
  - python
  - app.py

docs.title: Success Criteria
docs.text:
Your capstone is complete when:

  • all controls update the dashboard
  • the table and charts use the same filtered data
  • pipeline graph clicks update selected detail
  • kanban card clicks update selected detail
  • chat submit updates the assistant answer
  • the page has a branded theme
  • the app has Databricks-ready files

docs.title: Stretch Goals
docs.text:
• Add a Drawer for selected pipeline details.
  • Add a Modal for creating an action plan.
  • Replace mock data with Databricks SQL.
  • Add auth protection.
  • Add a second page for data model documentation.

docs.title: Checkpoint
docs.text: If you can build this capstone without copying the full example, you are no longer just trying BrickflowUI. You are building with it.

docs.title: App
docs.text: The db.App object that owns pages, routes, theme, state sessions, and runtime configuration.

docs.title: Component
docs.text: A Python function that returns a VNode. Examples include Text, Card, Table, and PipelineGraph.

docs.title: VNode
docs.text: The serializable representation of a UI node. BrickflowUI sends VNodes to the frontend.

docs.title: Page
docs.text: A Python function decorated with @app.page(...).

docs.title: State
docs.text: Session-specific data created with use_state.

docs.title: Event
docs.text: A browser interaction that calls a Python handler, such as button click, input change, table row click, or chat submit.

docs.title: Controlled Input
docs.text: An input whose displayed value comes from state and whose changes update that state.

docs.title: Patch
docs.text: A small UI update sent to the browser after state changes.

docs.title: Frontend Assets
docs.text: The built React frontend served from brickflowui/frontend/dist.

docs.title: MkDocs
docs.text: The documentation tool used by this project.

docs.title: Databricks App
docs.text: A hosted app runtime in Databricks that runs app.py using app.yaml and requirements.txt.

docs.title: Pipeline Graph
docs.text: A visual flow of nodes and edges representing a data pipeline or DAG.

docs.title: Capstone
docs.text: The final learning project that combines the major skills from the guide.
